front: What is the general form of a mixed ARMA(p, q) process?
back: A mixed autoregressive-moving average process of orders p and q, denoted ARMA(p,q), is defined by the equation: X_t = φ_1X_{t-1} + ... + φ_pX_{t-p} + Z_t + θ_1Z_{t-1} + ... + θ_qZ_{t-q}, where {Z_t} is a white noise process.

front: What is the key advantage of using an ARMA model over a pure AR or MA model?
back: ARMA models can often model a time series more parsimoniously, meaning they can capture complex autocorrelation patterns with fewer parameters than a pure high-order AR or MA model would require.

front: What is the necessary condition for an ARMA(p,q) process to be stationary?
back: The process is stationary if the roots of the autoregressive characteristic polynomial φ(z) = 1 - φ_1z - ... - φ_pz^p lie outside the unit circle (|z| > 1).

front: What is the necessary condition for an ARMA(p,q) process to be invertible?
back: The process is invertible if the roots of the moving average characteristic polynomial θ(z) = 1 + θ_1z + ... + θ_qz^q lie outside the unit circle (|z| > 1). This allows the ARMA model to be expressed as a pure AR(∞) model.

front: How can the AR and MA components of an ARMA model be intuitively interpreted?
back: The AR component (φ) models the dependency of the current value on its own past values (the 'internal' dynamics of the series). The MA component (θ) models the impact of past random shock values (the 'external' innovations) on the current value.
